title: Bokashi vs Traditional Composting: What's the Difference?
excerpt: Bokashi and traditional composting both reduce food waste, but they work in very different ways. Traditional composting creates finished compost through decomposition, while bokashi ferments waste first and relies on a second stage for full breakdown.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Comparing Methods

If you are interested in composting at home, you may come across two very different methods: bokashi composting and traditional composting. Both help reduce food waste and support healthier soil, but they work in completely different ways.

Traditional composting breaks organic matter down over time into finished compost. Bokashi uses fermentation to pre-process food waste before it is added to soil or another compost system. Because of that, the two methods suit different homes, spaces, and routines.

Understanding the difference can help you choose the one that fits your needs best.

What Is Traditional Composting?

Traditional composting is the method most people picture first. It involves combining organic materials like food scraps, dry leaves, cardboard, and garden waste in a pile or bin, then allowing them to decompose naturally.

This process depends on:

  • Microorganisms
  • Oxygen
  • Moisture
  • A balance of greens and browns

Over time, the materials break down into dark, crumbly compost that can be added directly to the garden.

Traditional composting can be done:

  • In an open pile
  • In a compost bin
  • In a tumbler
  • Outdoors in a backyard
  • In some cases with indoor methods like worm bins

What Is Bokashi Composting?

Bokashi composting is actually a fermentation process rather than standard composting. Food waste is placed in a sealed container and layered with bokashi bran, which contains beneficial microbes.

Instead of rotting, the food waste ferments in an oxygen-poor environment.

After this stage, the material is:

  • Buried in soil
  • Added to a traditional compost pile
  • Or placed in another system to finish breaking down

So bokashi does not usually create finished compost by itself. It creates a fermented pre-compost material that continues decomposing later.

The Main Difference

The biggest difference is simple:

Traditional composting decomposes waste into finished compost.
Bokashi ferments waste first, then the material finishes breaking down later.

That difference affects almost everything else, including what you can compost, how fast the process feels, how much space you need, and what kind of setup works best.

How Traditional Composting Works

Traditional composting relies on natural decomposition. You add ingredients, keep them balanced, and allow microbes and other decomposers to break them down over time.

A healthy traditional compost system needs:

  • Greens for nitrogen
  • Browns for carbon
  • Oxygen
  • Moisture
  • Time

Depending on the method, compost may take a few months or much longer.

How Bokashi Works

Bokashi uses a sealed bucket and bokashi bran to ferment food scraps. The process usually takes place indoors or in a compact space.

A typical bokashi system involves:

  1. Adding food waste to the bucket
  2. Sprinkling bokashi bran over each layer
  3. Keeping the container sealed
  4. Draining liquid if the bucket design allows it
  5. Letting the full bucket ferment
  6. Burying or composting the fermented material afterward

It is a two-stage process rather than a one-step composting method.

What You Can Add: One of the Biggest Differences

Traditional Composting

Most home compost systems work best with:

  • Fruit and vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Tea leaves
  • Eggshells
  • Dry leaves
  • Cardboard
  • Grass clippings
  • Garden trimmings

Many home composters avoid:

  • Meat
  • Dairy
  • Oily foods
  • Cooked leftovers

Bokashi

Bokashi can usually handle a wider range of kitchen waste, including:

  • Fruit and vegetables
  • Cooked food
  • Dairy
  • Meat in small household amounts
  • Bread
  • Rice
  • Pasta

This is one reason bokashi appeals to apartment dwellers or people who want to compost more of their kitchen waste.

Space Requirements

Traditional Composting

Traditional composting often needs more space, especially if you want a larger outdoor pile or bin. Even compact systems usually need enough room for airflow and some material volume.

Bokashi

Bokashi is much more compact. A small sealed bucket can fit in a kitchen, pantry, balcony, or utility area. That makes it especially attractive for apartments, condos, homes without yards, and small-space living.

Smell and Cleanliness

Traditional Composting

A well-managed compost pile should smell earthy, but if the balance is off, it can smell sour or rotten. Outdoor systems also need some care to avoid pests.

Bokashi

Because bokashi is done in a sealed container, it usually stays neater and more contained. When opened, it may have a sour fermented smell, but it should not smell like rotting garbage.

For indoor settings, bokashi often feels cleaner and easier to manage.

Speed of the Process

Traditional Composting

Traditional composting can be slow. Depending on the method, it may take a few months, half a year, or longer.

Bokashi

The fermentation stage is relatively quick, often taking only a couple of weeks once the bucket is full. But the waste is not finished compost at that point. It still needs time in soil or another compost system to break down fully.

So bokashi feels faster at first, but it does not always give you usable compost right away.

Finished Product

Traditional Composting

The final result is finished compost: dark, crumbly, earthy material that can go straight into garden beds.

Bokashi

The end of the bucket stage is not finished compost. It is fermented organic material that still looks partly recognizable. It must be buried, cured in soil, or added to another compost setup before plants can benefit from it fully.

This is one of the most important differences to understand.

Ease of Use

Traditional Composting

Traditional composting is straightforward, but it may require balancing greens and browns, turning the pile, managing moisture, and dealing with weather.

Bokashi

Bokashi is simple in a different way. It does not require turning or balancing browns and greens in the same way, but it does require bokashi bran, a sealed bucket, draining liquid in some systems, and a place to bury or finish the fermented waste.

Each method has its own learning curve.

Cost Differences

Traditional Composting

Traditional composting can be very cheap, especially with an open pile, a DIY bin, or reused materials.

Bokashi

Bokashi often costs a bit more because it usually requires a bokashi bucket, bokashi bran, and replacement bran over time.

The ongoing need for bran is one of the main added costs.

Which Method Is Better for Apartments?

For many apartment dwellers, bokashi is often easier than traditional composting because it:

  • Takes up little space
  • Stays sealed
  • Handles more food types
  • Works indoors

Traditional composting indoors is still possible, especially with worm bins, but bokashi is often easier for general food scrap collection in a small home.

Which Method Is Better for Backyards?

For many people with a yard, traditional composting is the more practical long-term option because it:

  • Creates finished compost directly
  • Handles garden waste well
  • Does not require special bran
  • Scales up more easily

If you already have outdoor compost space, traditional composting is usually the simpler system overall.

Can You Use Both Together?

Yes, and many people do.

A very effective combination is:

  • Use bokashi indoors for daily food waste
  • Then add the fermented material to an outdoor compost pile or bury it in the garden

This approach lets you compost a wider range of kitchen scraps while still producing finished compost through a second stage.

So Which One Should You Choose?

Choose traditional composting if you:

  • Have outdoor space
  • Want finished compost directly
  • Compost yard waste
  • Prefer a low-cost long-term system

Choose bokashi if you:

  • Live in a small space
  • Want an indoor method
  • Want to compost more food types
  • Need a clean, sealed system

The better method depends on your home, your waste, and what kind of process feels manageable.

Final Thoughts

Bokashi and traditional composting both reduce waste, but they do it in very different ways. Traditional composting is a decomposition process that creates finished compost. Bokashi is a fermentation method that prepares food waste for later breakdown.

If you have outdoor space and want a classic compost system, traditional composting is often the better choice. If you need a compact indoor setup and want to handle more kitchen waste, bokashi may be the better fit.

Both methods can be useful. The best one is the one that works with your space and daily routine.
